semantic_journey: Arabic sajara = to fill watercourses/flood channels (the root s-j-r = to fill/flood). Greek Sangarios = a river name (Sakarya River in Anatolia). The original reasoning: filling watercourses -> river name. While rivers fill their courses, the Greek proper name Sangarios is a specific river toponym of Phrygian/Anatolian origin, not derived from an Arabic root. Proper name (river toponym).
key_insight: Greek Sangarios is an Anatolian/Phrygian river name - not derived from Arabic filling-watercourses concept.

semantic_journey: Arabic sakhata = settling/sinking, subsiding into a state of rest (the root s-kh-t = to sink/settle/subside). Greek hesukhazetai = be quiet, rest, be at peace (from hesukhos = quiet/still). Both encode the transition from activity to quietness/stillness. Arabic: settling/subsiding; Greek: being quiet/at rest. One clear step: settling/subsiding -> quieting/resting.
key_insight: Arabic settling/subsiding (sakhata) and Greek being-quiet/resting (hesukhazetai) share the stillness concept.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-qubrus = Cyprus (the island), explicitly associated with copper (the island that gave copper its name - kupros = copper). Greek khalkobaros = weighed down with bronze/copper (khalko = bronze/copper + baros = weight/burden). Both encode the copper/bronze concept. Arabic: Cyprus as the island of copper; Greek: loaded with copper/bronze. One step: the copper-island (Cyprus) -> heavy with copper/bronze. The semantic core of copper is shared.
key_insight: Arabic Cyprus/copper island (al-qubrus) and Greek copper-heavy (khalkobaros) share the copper/bronze semantic core.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-binyth = a type of deep-sea fish (named with the letters b-y-n-th). Greek benthos = depth (of the sea). The original reasoning: deep-sea fish -> the depth of the sea. However, Arabic al-binyth names a specific fish; Greek benthos names the depth itself. These are different categories (animal vs location). Also noted: Arabic borrowed the Greek term for deep-sea fish. If Arabic borrowed from Greek, this is Greek-to-Arabic, not the reverse.
key_insight: Arabic deep-sea fish (al-binyth) and Greek sea-depth (benthos) are different categories; Arabic likely borrowed from Greek.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-taghran = boiling/effervescence (also as a proper name). Greek Tigranes = an Armenian royal name. Tigranes is an Iranian/Armenian proper name (from Old Iranian Tigra + -ana suffix). Arabic al-taghran as a proper name may phonetically resemble Tigranes, but there is no etymological connection between Arabic boiling/effervescence and the Armenian royal name. Proper name coincidence.
key_insight: Greek Tigranes is an Iranian/Armenian royal name - no connection to Arabic boiling/effervescence root.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-tantalum = short/small person. Greek Tantalos = Tantalus (the king in Greek mythology punished with eternal hunger/thirst). Tantalus is a Greek mythological proper name. The connection proposed through diminishment/lacking is a mythological interpretation not an etymological one. Proper name - excluded.
key_insight: Greek Tantalus is a mythological proper name - not etymologically connected to Arabic small/diminished person.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-sharshaq = a loose/promiscuous woman (a Semitic root describing a woman of loose morals/licentiousness). Greek kaseoris = strumpet/prostitute. Both directly mean a sexually loose/promiscuous woman. The semantic match is exact: Arabic names the behavior (looseness/promiscuity) and Greek names the role (prostitute). One direct step: sexually loose woman -> prostitute. This is a genuine direct semantic preservation.
key_insight: Arabic loose/promiscuous woman (al-sharshaq) and Greek strumpet/prostitute (kaseoris) are exact semantic equivalents.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-samandal = salamander (the mythical fire creature, also the word for a fire-resistant material). Greek salamandra = salamander. Both name the same mythological fire-creature/actual salamander. However, Arabic al-samandal is borrowed FROM Greek salamandra (or from a shared Eastern source that also gave Greek the word). The direction is Greek-to-Arabic, not Arabic-to-Greek. Shared loanword from a third source or Greek-to-Arabic.
key_insight: Arabic salamander borrowed from Greek or shared Eastern source - not a native Arabic root cognate for Greek.
